claim: You need a passport to vote
explanation: In most democracies, a government-issued photo ID such as a driver's licence or national ID card is sufficient. A passport is one of several accepted forms of ID.

claim: Voting is not compulsory
explanation: Compulsory voting varies by country. Australia has mandatory voting with fines for non-compliance. Most countries treat voting as a right, not an obligation.

claim: You can vote multiple times by going to different booths
explanation: Electoral rolls are marked and cross-checked in real time. Attempting to vote more than once is electoral fraud and a criminal offence.

claim: Online voting is available everywhere
explanation: Only a handful of countries (e.g. Estonia) have national online voting. Most democracies require in-person attendance or postal voting.

claim: Postal votes are not counted
explanation: Postal votes are legally valid and counted alongside in-person votes, though counting may occur after election day in some jurisdictions.

claim: If you spoil your ballot it gets counted as a vote for the incumbent
explanation: Spoilt ballots are recorded as informal votes and do not count toward any candidate. They are separately tallied and reported.

claim: You can take photos of your ballot
explanation: Laws vary by jurisdiction. In many countries photographing your completed ballot is prohibited to prevent vote-selling. Check your local electoral authority.

claim: Candidates can watch the vote counting
explanation: Accredited scrutineers representing candidates and parties are legally permitted to observe the counting process.

claim: Election results are decided before counting finishes
explanation: Preliminary results are projections based on counted votes. Official results are only certified after all votes including postal and overseas votes are counted.

claim: Only citizens can vote
explanation: In most countries, only citizens can vote in national elections. Some countries allow permanent residents to vote in local elections.

claim: You can be paid to vote for a particular candidate
explanation: Vote-buying is illegal in all democracies and constitutes electoral fraud. Both the buyer and seller can face criminal prosecution.

claim: Your vote is not secret
explanation: The secret ballot is a cornerstone of democratic elections. Ballot papers are not linked to voter identities after being cast.

claim: If you don't vote nothing bad happens
explanation: In countries with compulsory voting, not voting results in fines. In others, not voting means your voice is not represented, which can affect election outcomes.

claim: The media declares the winner before all votes are counted
explanation: Media organisations make projections based on counted votes and exit polls, but only the official electoral authority can legally declare a winner.

claim: Election officials can change your vote
explanation: Physical ballots are secured and counted by multiple officials under scrutiny from all parties. Electronic systems have audit trails. Tampering is a serious crime.

claim: You must vote for every position on the ballot
explanation: Requirements vary by jurisdiction and ballot type. Some require preferences for all candidates (full preferential); others allow partial voting (optional preferential).

claim: Social media polls predict election results accurately
explanation: Social media polls have significant self-selection bias and are not representative of the voting population. They are not reliable predictors of election outcomes.
sources: American Association for Public Opinion Research

claim: Overseas voters cannot vote
explanation: Most democracies provide mechanisms for citizens living abroad to vote, including postal voting, proxy voting, or in-person voting at embassies.

claim: All votes carry equal weight
explanation: In first-past-the-post systems, votes in safe seats carry less practical weight. Proportional representation systems aim for more equal weighting. Electoral systems vary.

claim: You need to register to vote every election
explanation: In many countries, automatic or permanent registration means you only register once. In others, annual registration is required. Check your local electoral authority.

claim: Dead people are still on the electoral roll
explanation: Electoral authorities regularly audit and update rolls, but delays can occur. Voter rolls are not used in isolation — officials cross-check identity before issuing ballots.

claim: Electronic voting machines can be hacked easily
explanation: Modern voting systems use air-gapped networks, paper audit trails, and multi-party testing. No large-scale successful hack of certified voting machines has been documented.
